---
title: "TODO Report an LMM"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
source('../assets/setup.R')
```


In DAPR2, you learned to write an Analysis Plan section followed by a Results section.
You might have noticed, though, that published academic articles typically go straight from a Methods section (where they discuss the study design, the study procedure, their participants, and the materials) into a Results section (where they report what they found).
In DAPR3, we will train you to write something more like an article-style Results section.

This flashcard doesn't contain an example write-up.
We want you to write your own report instead of using ours as a template.
Below you'll find guidelines and checklists for you to follow, so that you know you've included all the important information.


## Checklist for a strong Results section

**Set the scene (so the reader knows what you're talking about).**

- [ ] Remind the reader briefly what the study aimed to test (e.g., what the predictions were).
- [ ] Explain what factors the study is manipulating and what the outcome variable is.


**Describe and show the sample data (so that the reader knows how generalisable your analysis might be).**

- [ ] Mention any data cleaning / outlier removal you did before computing the descriptives.
- [ ] Describe the outcome and predictor variables in your data (e.g., what scale are they on? how were they measured?)
- [ ] Describe the grouping variables in your data (see [Describe group-structured data](describe.html)).
  - Tip: Tables and plots are easier to understand than listing loads of numbers in the text (and better for your word count too!). 
  - Tip: Plots are easier to understand than tables of numbers.
  
  
**Summarise and visualise the results that address the RQ (so the reader knows what to expect).**

- [ ] In a sentence or two, describe the overall pattern of results.
- [ ] Include a plot of the data (and make sure to refer to it in the text).
  - Tip: If you transform any continuous predictors for analysis, use the transformed version of the predictor in the plot.
  

**Introduce your statistical analysis (so the reader can interpret the numbers you will report).**

- [ ] Describe the fixed effects of your model, e.g.: "We fit a linear mixed model predicting Y as a function of X1, X2, and their interaction".
- [ ] If fitting a GLM, describe the model family and the link function, e.g.: "This binomial model used the logit link".
- [ ] If categorical predictors, describe contrast coding, e.g.: "X1 has two levels, A (coded as –1) and B (coded as 1)".
- [ ] If continuous predictors have been transformed, describe the transformation, e.g.: "X2 has been z-scored".
- [ ] Describe the random effect structure of the [maximal model](maxmodel.html).
  - One option (more traditional, less transparent): "The maximal model includes by-participant random intercepts, by-participant random slopes over X1, and by-stimulus random intercepts."
  - Another option (less traditional, more transparent): "The maximal model includes by-participant adjustments to the intercept and to the slope over X1, as well as by-stimulus intercept adjustments."
- [ ] If you had to [simplify](simplify.html) the random effect structure, describe the order in which you removed parameters.
- [ ] Describe the random effect structure of the actual model that you will report.
- [ ] State the software packages and versions used to fit models, along with the estimation method (ML/REML), how p-values were approximated (probably Satterthwaite), what optimiser you used... 
- [ ] State which model coefficient(s) are related to which prediction(s), and what direction/sign (positive or negative) you would expect the coefficients to have if the predictions were borne out.


**Report the results of your analysis (so the reader knows what you found).**

- [ ] State the key coefficient estimates along with their 95% CI and p-value, e.g.: "The slope associating X1 and Y was significantly different from zero ($b$ = 0.26, 95% CI [0.23, 0.29], $p$ < 0.001)".
  - Tip: You don't need to report every single parameter estimate. Focus on the one(s) you identified as being relevant to your prediction(s).
- [ ] Report the magnitude of the SD of the group-level adjustments and compare it to the magnitude of the fixed effects. How does the intercept vary between groups? How does the effect of interest vary between groups?
- [ ] Report the correlations between intercept and slope adjustments, if relevant.
- [ ] Plot the fitted model estimates, including group-level estimates (see [Plot model-fitted values](plot-fitted.html)).
- [ ] Report any other information you consider important for your story!


## General guidelines

- Your golden guiding principle should be: **does the reader have all the information they would need to replicate your analysis in whatever statistical software they might be using?**

- The goal of a Results section is to **tell the reader what you found.** 
The goal of the Discussion section is to tell the reader what it all means. 
The actual *interpretation* of the results belongs in the Discussion section, not the Results section.

- Follow the [APA guidelines on how to report numbers and statistics](https://apastyle.apa.org/instructional-aids/numbers-statistics-guide.pdf).

- If your plot contains error bars, tell the reader what those error bars represent (standard deviation? standard error? 95% CI?).

- To make tables look extra professional, use horizontal lines only.





## Linked flash cards

### Outgoing links

- TODO


### Backlinks

- TODO
